title: Two-Tier Routing, Backpressure Engine, and Conversation-Mediated Dispatch
intention: When this cluster is complete, a developer configures routing as a declaration -- predicate subscriptions for the common case, routing functions for the flexible case -- and the bus handles the rest. Backpressure is not an afterthought bolted onto the consumer; it is a protocol primitive that producers see and react to in real time. Consumer groups do not rebalance. A dispatch conversation selects a consumer, links to it, observes completion, and re-routes on failure -- per message, with sub-millisecond crash detection via beamr process links. The experience should feel like the bus is an intelligent intermediary that understands both what messages mean and how much work consumers can absorb, rather than a dumb pipe that delivers bytes and hopes for the best.
problem:
Existing messaging systems split routing and backpressure into separate, disconnected concerns. NATS uses subject wildcards for routing and has no backpressure at all -- slow consumers simply drop messages. Kafka uses partition keys for routing and consumer-lag monitoring for backpressure, but producers cannot react in real time because lag is a trailing indicator. RabbitMQ has exchange types for routing and per-consumer prefetch for backpressure, but prefetch is coarse and not content-aware. In all cases: routing is static (declared at subscription time, no content inspection beyond key matching), backpressure is reactive rather than preventive, and consumer group rebalancing is operationally disruptive.

Kafka's consumer group rebalancing is the sharpest pain. Adding or removing a consumer triggers a stop-the-world partition reassignment that pauses all consumers in the group. NATS queue groups are simpler but still lack per-message routing control. Neither system can make a load-aware dispatch decision on a per-message basis.

The liminal bus needs routing that is content-aware (predicate matching on message fields), extensible (arbitrary Gleam logic for load-aware or state-dependent decisions), and integrated with pressure (routing decisions consider consumer capacity). It needs backpressure that is explicit (typed signals in the wire protocol), proactive (producers know before overload, not after), and policy-driven (the bus enforces configurable responses). And it needs consumer dispatch that is per-message and fault-aware, not per-partition and rebalance-prone.
solution:
The routing cluster delivers three interlocking subsystems: the routing engine, the backpressure engine, and conversation-mediated dispatch.

1. Two-Tier Routing Engine

Routing operates on two tiers unified by a single runtime model per ADR-003. Predicate subscriptions handle the 90% case: a consumer subscribes to a channel with a WHERE clause consisting of field comparisons (equality, inequality, greater-than, less-than, range), boolean combinators (AND, OR, NOT), and field-existence checks. The bus evaluates predicates at publish time against message fields, applies short-circuit evaluation and index-aware field extraction ordering, and delivers only to matching consumers. Predicates are declarative, analyzable, and optimizable.

Routing functions handle the 10% case: Gleam code compiled to BEAM bytecode, loaded into beamr via content-hash module loading, and executed as supervised processes. A routing function receives the message and a view of consumer state -- capacity, affinity tags, recent activity -- and returns a routing decision. Functions can implement load-aware dispatch, session affinity, A/B routing, and any logic predicates cannot express.

The unification: predicates compile to routing functions via the compiler module. The routing engine always executes functions. Predicates are syntactic sugar that produce optimized, indexable functions. One evaluation path, one supervision model, one hot-deployment mechanism.

The routing table maps channels to their active subscriptions. When a message is published, the table resolves matching subscriptions and dispatches accordingly. Subscription registration and removal do not block concurrent routing evaluations.

2. Backpressure Engine

Backpressure is a protocol primitive per ADR-004. Three signals flow from consumer to bus to producer: accept (message delivered and processing begun), defer (message buffered, will deliver when capacity frees), reject (consumer overwhelmed, message shed). Consumers declare capacity at subscription time: max in-flight messages and max buffer depth.

The pressure monitor tracks per-consumer and per-channel metrics: current in-flight count, buffer depth, accept/defer/reject rates, and a derived pressure score. These metrics feed both the routing engine (pressure-aware dispatch) and the policy engine.

Pressure policies are per-channel configurations governing automatic responses to overload. Four policy actions: slow-producer (signal producer to reduce rate), shed-load (reject lowest-priority messages), scale-consumer (emit scaling signal for external orchestration), alert (emit pressure event for observability). Policies compose: a channel can slow producers first, then shed load if pressure persists, with escalation thresholds controlling the progression.

3. Conversation-Mediated Dispatch

Per ADR-007, consumer groups do not use Kafka-style rebalancing. A dispatch conversation is a beamr process that receives a message, evaluates the group's routing function to select a consumer, links to the selected consumer process, forwards the message, and observes completion. If the consumer crashes, the link notifies the dispatch conversation immediately -- sub-millisecond via beamr process links, not heartbeat-interval -- and it re-routes to another consumer.

Consumer groups are modeled as a routing function over a consumer set. Adding or removing a consumer updates the set; the next dispatch conversation sees the change immediately. No stop-the-world rebalance, no partition reassignment, no paused consumers.

The dispatch conversation also mediates backpressure: if the selected consumer defers, the conversation can try another consumer or defer upstream. The dispatch conversation is the integration point where routing decisions and pressure signals meet.

Module Layout

The routing subsystem lives under crates/liminal/src/routing/ with modules for predicate types, evaluation, compilation, routing function execution, the routing table, dispatch logic, and consumer groups. The pressure subsystem lives under crates/liminal/src/pressure/ with modules for signal types, capacity tracking, policy configuration, pressure monitoring, and policy enforcement. Both directories follow CN3: mod.rs contains only pub mod and pub use.

principles.text: Predicates are functions. The two routing tiers share one runtime model. Predicates compile to functions; the engine always executes functions. No special-case code paths for predicates versus functions.

Pressure is observable and typed. Backpressure signals are explicit protocol primitives with defined semantics, not inferred from lag counters or queue depths. Producers receive typed feedback on every publish.
Dispatch is per-message, not per-partition. Consumer selection happens on every message via the dispatch conversation. No global state, no rebalance protocol, no stop-the-world.
Routing and pressure are integrated. Routing functions can inspect consumer pressure. Pressure policies can alter routing behavior. The two subsystems share state, not just co-exist.
Crash detection is structural. Consumer failure is detected via beamr process links in sub-millisecond time, not via heartbeat timeouts. The dispatch conversation observes failure directly.
Policies are declarative, enforcement is automatic. Channel owners declare pressure policies; the bus enforces them without application code. Overload handling is a bus responsibility.
Hot deployment for routing logic. Routing functions load via content-hash module loading and update without restarting the bus or dropping connections.

goals: Predicate subscriptions with field comparisons, boolean combinators, and range checks evaluate correctly against published messages and deliver only to matching consumers.
Routing functions written in Gleam execute as supervised beamr processes with fault isolation -- a crashing routing function does not affect routing evaluations for other channels.
Consumers declaring max in-flight and buffer depth receive accept, defer, or reject signals on every message delivery, accurately reflecting capacity state.
Pressure policies (slow-producer, shed-load, scale-consumer, alert) trigger automatically when per-channel pressure thresholds are crossed.
Conversation-mediated dispatch selects a consumer, links to it, and re-routes to another consumer within one millisecond of detecting a consumer crash via process link.
Adding or removing a consumer from a group takes effect on the next dispatch without pausing or rebalancing other consumers in the group.

non_goals.text: Persistence of routing state across restarts
non_goals.reason: Routing tables and subscriptions are reconstructed from consumer re-registration. Durable subscription state belongs to the durability cluster (RM-003).

non_goals.text: Multi-node routing and cross-node consumer groups
non_goals.reason: Single-node routing first. Multi-node dispatch requires the distribution protocol, which is a separate concern.

non_goals.text: Custom predicate query language or SQL-like parser
non_goals.reason: Predicates are structured types (field, operator, value), not parsed from strings. A query language can be layered on top in the SDK cluster.
The pressure policy emits a scale-consumer signal for external orchestration (Aion, Kubernetes). The bus does not spawn consumers itself.

non_goals.text: Message priority queuing beyond pressure-based shedding
non_goals.reason: Pressure policies can shed by priority class, but priority-ordered delivery within a channel is not in scope for this cluster.

structure.note: Routing module root: pub mod and pub use only
Predicate types: field comparisons, boolean combinators, range checks
Predicate evaluation engine: match predicates against borrowed message fields
Routing table: channel-to-subscription registry, concurrent-safe updates
Predicate-to-function compiler: predicates become optimized routing functions
Routing function execution: load and run Gleam routing functions in beamr
Conversation-mediated dispatch: consumer selection, linking, failure re-routing
Consumer group model: consumer set management, group routing function
Pressure module root: pub mod and pub use only
Backpressure signal types: accept, defer, reject
Consumer capacity declaration and tracking: max in-flight, buffer depth
Pressure policy configuration: slow-producer, shed-load, scale-consumer, alert
Pressure monitor: per-consumer and per-channel metrics, pressure score derivation
Policy enforcement: evaluate policies against monitor state, trigger actions

constraints.text: No file over 500 lines. If a module approaches this limit, extract into submodules.
No panics in production code. .unwrap() and .expect() only in tests.
mod.rs contains ONLY pub mod declarations and pub use re-exports. No logic.
All public types implement Debug. All error types implement std::error::Error.
Predicate evaluation must not allocate per-message. Predicates evaluate against borrowed message fields with no heap allocation on the hot path.
Backpressure signals must be delivered to producers within the same publish round-trip. No asynchronous notification after the fact.
Consumer crash detection via process link must trigger re-routing within one millisecond. No polling or heartbeat-based detection.
Routing function execution is supervised and isolated. A panic or infinite loop in one routing function must not block evaluation for other channels.
Adding or removing a consumer from a group must not pause, block, or restart dispatch for other consumers in the same group.
